original_abstract:
This paper examines the logistical, legal, and professional complexities inherent in international higher education collaborations, specifically focusing on the rise and regulation of joint and double-degree programs. The first part of the analysis details the systemic barriers to these programs, including conflicting academic calendars, disparate accreditation standards, and the "vexing" legal issues surrounding the recognition of multi-institutional qualifications. It highlights the debate over the academic legitimacy of "double counting" credits and emphasizes the need for a global consensus on program definitions and quality assurance. 

Complementing this structural overview, the paper incorporates data from the 2007/08 Open Doors survey to analyze shifting trends in international student mobility in the United States. Following a post-9/11 decline, enrollment reached record highs, driven largely by a surge in graduate students from India, China, and South Korea, as well as new scholarship initiatives in the Middle East. Finally, the discussion underscores the critical role of a full-time, well-supported faculty in maintaining institutional quality. By highlighting the risks of an aging professoriate and the rise of part-time "taxicab" professors, the paper argues that the success of internationalization and higher education at large depends fundamentally on investing in human capital rather than just physical infrastructure.
humanized_abstract:
Papers in this thematic collection illuminate various aspects of the complexity of international collaboration in higher education (HE). Against the backdrop of increasingly widespread involvement in transnational HE partnerships, this paper discusses challenges faced by joint/double degree programs resulting from combinations of logistical, regulatory and professional factors. The article discusses structural obstacles including differences in semester patterns, requirements for degree recognition and solvable, but vexing, problems related to the recognition of qualifications that involve more than two HE institutions. It discusses the "double-counting" controversy and highlights the urgent need for a worldwide consensu on degree definitions and quality assurance procedures in the context of collaborative programmes like joint/double degree arrangements.

As an overview of the physical structures that must be in place for an international program to flourish, this paper serves a broader purpose by incorporating data from the 2007/08 Open Doors survey of the International Student and Scholar Exchange and Viewing Program, a program of the Institute of International Education. (See Table 1, “International Students in U.S. Higher Education” in Appendix A.) Although international students have fallen as a percentage of total college enrollment since the post-9/11 decline of students from countries other than Canada, total numbers remain high. Enrollee numbers soared in particular among graduate students from India, China, and South Korea, as well as among students receiving new scholarships given by governments in the Middle East. The paper ultimately emphasizes the importance of full-time faculty, adequately housed and supported, to a successful international program and to quality in higher education generally, and cautions against the deterioration of the faculty as an aging body and an increasing number of part-time faculty, or what has been termed “taxicab” faculty, threaten academic quality and undermine all internationalization efforts in higher education.
